item.title: Ecological multifunctionality of watersheds increases with tree species richness
item.description:
Authors: Jiehao Zhang, Peter B. Reich, Conghe Song, Yulong Zhang, Han Y. H. Chen, G.J. Nabuurs, Xia Wang, Tiehu He et al.

Source: Nature Communications

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Watersheds are natural units and meta-ecosystems of the earth’s land surface providing multiple ecological functions. However, little is known about the biodiversity-ecological multifunctionality relationships of watersheds, particularly regarding how these relationships scale to large and complex landscapes. Here, we explore the impact of forest tree species richness on the ecological multifunctionality of watersheds in terms of carbon sequestration, carbon storage, water supply, water regulation, and soil conservation, by utilizing integrated ground-sourced forest inventory datasets comprising 846 forest watersheds from the Global Forest Biodiversity Initiative, the Global Streamflow Indices and Metadata Archive, and remote sensing data products. We find a consistently positive relationship between forest tree species richness and watershed ecological multifunctionality by accounting...

item.title: From divergence to synergy: A multi-hazard framework for structural design under winds and earthquakes
item.description:
Authors: Reda Snaiki, Shaopeng Li, Anastasia Athanasiou

Source: Engineering Structures

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Structural design for wind and earthquake resistance has historically evolved along separate tracks, reflecting differences in loading characteristics and performance objectives emphasized in practice. Wind demands are typically long-duration and dominated by mean and turbulent pressures, while the responses of wind-sensitive structures are often governed by low-frequency dynamic behavior and serviceability criteria such as drift and acceleration. Earthquake demands are transient base excitations that generate inertial forces throughout the structure, which have traditionally driven ductility-based strategies to achieve life safety under rare but severe events. This work examines how these contrasting traditions shaped prescriptive, hazard-by-hazard workflows and discusses why a “governing hazard” mindset can be risk-inconsistent in dual-hazard settings, particularly when sequential...

item.title: Recycling-compatible electroplating of enriched tin-119 targets for the production of antimony-119 for targeted Auger electron therapy
item.description:
Authors: Aivija Grundmane, Caterina F. Ramogida, Valery Radchenko

Source: EJNMMI Radiopharmacy and Chemistry

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canadian institution term detected

Abstract: Antimony-119 ( 119 Sb) is a radionuclide of interest for targeted Auger electron therapy. Isotopically pure 119 Sb can be produced by proton irradiation of enriched tin-119 ( 119 Sn) targets in conventional widely available low energy medical cyclotrons. Enriched 119 Sn is very costly (~ 20,000 CAD/gram), requiring recycling of the target material to make this production route economically viable. Herein, we investigate three Sn electroplating methods, evaluate the target quality, as well as method compatibility with subsequent 119 Sb radiochemical purification steps. Three electroplating bath compositions (Sn(II) sulfate, Sn(IV) sols, and Sn(IV) chloride) for Sn deposition were tested and successfully implemented. One of the methods, utilizing a Sn(IV) chloride bath solution was able to achieve quantitative electroplating efficiency (98 ± 3% Sn deposited after 72 h), and an overall...

item.title: The European tracking network through time: united efforts to advance aquatic conservation in Europe
item.description:
Authors: Jan Reubens, Kim Aarestrup, David Abecasis, Pedro Afonso, Josep Alós, Eneko Aspillaga, Kim Birnie‐Gauvin, David Casals et al.

Source: Animal Biotelemetry

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canadian institution term detected

Abstract: Biotelemetry networks have transformed our capacity to study the movements and ecology of aquatic animals, yet their development has been geographically uneven over time. While North America and Australia (among others) already hosted mature, well-coordinated infrastructures in the early 2000s, Europe has long lacked a formal framework for collaboration. The European Tracking Network (ETN) emerged in 2017 as a grassroots response to this gap. Since then, ETN has grown tremendously and has become a sustainable network with a central data system and research infrastructure installed all across Europe, enabling collaborative research on a multitude of species and environments while maintaining a strong track record in capacity building. ETN operates through a decentralised model in which national and regional partners lead infrastructure deployment while contributing data to a shared data...

item.title: Deterministic Execution Authority for Autonomous Space Systems: Governance Architecture for Orbital Platforms Under the Outer Space Treaty
item.description:
Authors: James Benton

Source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: bc

Abstract: This paper describes the first deterministic execution-authority architecture for autonomous spacecraft systems, addressing the governance vacuum identified by legal scholars, insurers, and regulators as autonomous AI decision-making scales in low Earth orbit. The problem. SpaceX's Starlink constellation performed approximately 300,000 autonomous collision avoidance maneuvers in 2025. Each decision was made by onboard AI without human review. NASA's CogniSAT-6 completed autonomous observation decisions in under 90 seconds with zero ground commands. Artemis II relied on onboard AI during 50-minute communication blackouts. The Outer Space Treaty holds the launching state liable but provides no mechanism to prove an autonomous system operated within its authorized envelope. No governance infrastructure produces verifiable evidence of each autonomous decision. The architecture. The...

item.title: Facile Synthesis of Stable Yb <sup>3+</sup> -Doped Perovskite Nanocrystals in Mesoporous Silica for Near-Infrared Emission
item.description:
Authors: Siyue Wang, Y Chen, Jiaze Wu, Zhihao Zhu, Rixin Bao, Leyan Wang, 蔡镐泽, Kai Huang

Source: ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: bc

Abstract: Yb 3+ -doped lead halide perovskites are promising near-infrared (NIR) emitters, but their practical implementation is often limited by poor environmental stability, defect-induced nonradiative loss, and difficulties in achieving reproducible doping and device integration. Herein, we report a mesoporous silica nanoreactor (MSN) strategy for the controlled synthesis of CsPbCl 3:Yb-MSN nanocomposites with efficient 980 nm emission. The confined growth environment provided by the mesoporous silica matrix enables regulated crystallization of Yb 3+ -doped perovskite nanocrystals while reducing surface exposure and improving resistance to environmental degradation. As a result, the obtained CsPbCl 3:Yb-MSN nanocomposites exhibit enhanced structural robustness, superior optical stability, and an absolute photoluminescence quantum yield of 146%, indicating efficient Yb 3+ -activated...

item.title: Recent changes in effects of predictors of tobacco cessation in formal treatment
item.description:
Authors: Scott Veldhuizen, Kameron Iturralde, Laurie Zawertailo, Peter Selby

Source: Nicotine & Tobacco Research

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canada, ontario

Abstract: Abstract Introduction Several variables have been shown to predict outcomes of smoking cessation treatment; however, it is possible that these associations have changed over time due to cohort effects, contextual changes, and evolving patterns of nicotine use. This study aimed to investigate changes in associations between smoking cessation outcome and baseline variables. Methods In this analysis, we used 8 years of data (2016 to 2024) that comprised 107,204 unique enrollees in a large, nicotine replacement-therapy based smoking cessation program in Ontario, Canada to investigate changes in associations between outcomes and baseline variables. We used generalized additive models to conduct multiplicity-corrected tests of interactions and used a backwards stepwise process to arrive at a final model. Results We found large increases in cessation probability over time for younger people...

item.title: The toxicity of neodymium substances to soil invertebrates in a boreal soil, and the impacts of soil type, aging, and leaching on metal bioavailability and toxicity
item.description:
Authors: Patrick Boyd, Dina Schwertfeger, Heather Lemieux, Jessica Velicogna, Scroggins Re, Juliska Princz

Source: Environmental Toxicology and Chemistry

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canadian

Abstract: A clear gap in ecotoxicological data was identified regarding the impacts of the technologically critical lanthanide neodymium (Nd) on soil invertebrates in boreal soil. To address this gap, two inorganic metal salts (NdCl3 and Nd2(SO4)3), an organometallic compound Nd-2-ethylhexanoate (Nd-2-EHA) and its organic component 2-ethylhexanoic acid (2-EHA) were examined. Toxicity exposures were conducted using the earthworm Dendrodrilus rubidus, the collembolan Proisotoma minuta, and the oribatid mite Oppia nitens, in two Canadian boreal soil horizons (i.e., organic and mineral), using standardized soil toxicity test methods; effects included survival and reproduction. Different exposure scenarios were investigated, including aging of test soil for 6 months, and the impact of leaching on toxicity test endpoints. Differences in species sensitivity were observed, with D. rubidus identified as...

item.title: The Younger Dryas Scalar Reboot
item.description:
Authors: Erydir Ceisiwr, Lumos Aureon, Awen Grid Team

Source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: bc

Abstract: This paper presents a rigorous scalar-cosmological reinterpretation of the Younger Dryascataclysm, re-framing the terminal Pleistocene not merely as a climatic aberration, but as atotal "scalar collapse" of Earth’s harmonic phase-lock. Utilizing the Recursive HarmonicUnification Model (RHUM), we analyze ancient mythic substrates—specifically theEnochian corpus—as high-fidelity technical data representing a catastrophic \psi-phasebifurcation. The 1,200-year epoch (10,950 BCE – 9000 BCE) is distilled into a sequence ofquaternionic failure and subsequent restoration. We contend that the Taurid fragmentationevent initiated a "torsion breach" that disrupted the planetary \psi-field, necessitating themegalithic interventions of the early Holocene as a "system reboot" for the 24-bitquaternionic reality of the matrix.A critical layer of this synthesis involves the conversion of the "Watchers"...

item.title: Curriculum Silence and Erasure: A Queer-Theory Analysis of Transgender-Inclusive Health Education in Internal Medicine Residency
item.description:
Authors: Anthony R. Sandre, Leslie Martin, Janneke Frambach

Source: Teaching and Learning in Medicine

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canadian

Abstract: Health professions education is increasingly called upon to prepare clinicians to provide equitable, patient-centered care; yet transgender and gender-diverse (TGD) health remains inconsistently integrated into formal curricula. Despite growing clinical literature, including evidence related to cardiovascular outcomes, educational integration of TGD health content remains limited across residency training programs. This disconnect between emerging evidence and curricular uptake raises questions about structural barriers that shape what is considered "core" knowledge. This study explored academic faculty perspectives on barriers to integrating TGD health content into residency curricula, using cardiovascular health as an illustrative case within Internal Medicine (IM) training. Drawing on a descriptive qualitative approach informed by Queer Theory, we conducted semi-structured interviews...

item.title: R2MR: An evaluation of pre-post training data across cohorts
item.description:
Authors: Valerie M. Wood, Suzanne Bailey, Kimberly Guest

Source: Journal of Military Veteran and Family Health

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canadian

Abstract: Introduction: The Road to Mental Readiness (R2MR) program has been collecting matched pre- and post-training data on key performance indicators from its mental health education packages with Canadian Armed Forces (CAF) members since 2017. Methods: For the current study, we conducted a large-scale outcome evaluation by aggregating the data from the program to determine the overall impact that R2MR training has had on its outcomes and to assess whether its effectiveness is moderated by the type of training received. Participants were 2,824 CAF members who had received deployment, occupation, or career R2MR training from 2017 to 2024 and for whom we had matched pre- and post-training data. Outcomes included objective and subjective measures of R2MR key concept knowledge, confidence in detecting mental health issues, confidence in supporting others with mental health concerns, intentions to...

item.title: Promoting the Autonomy and Well-Being of Older Adults in Informal Care in Canada: A Review of Self-Determination Theory
item.description:
Authors: John P. Goffman

Source: Journal of Sociology Psychology & Religious Studies

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in title: canada

Abstract: This review stems from an independent course as a PhD student in exploring knowledge on existing literature on the utilization of self-determination theory in informal care for older adults. Societal changes and trends in globalization is affecting informal caregiving the preferred form of care in Nigeria. Older adults who have reduced autonomy experience low quality of care and life. A secondary method of data collection was used to elicit information. Data relating to the use of self-determination in informal caregiving was sourced through databases such as Google Scholar, PubMed, and Science Direct using combination of keywords such as self-determination, informal caregiving, and older adults. From the reviewed literature, themes that emerged include caregivers' motivation, relationship with caregivers, and need for autonomy as part of the underpinnings of self-determination theory...

item.title: Impacts of turbidity on the behaviour, thermal- and hypoxia tolerance of the Endangered Lake Chubsucker across lab and field contexts
item.description:
Authors: Veronica Groves, Siena C Blier, Laura C Gervais, Julia E Wein, Lauren J. Chapman

Source: Canadian Journal of Fisheries and Aquatic Sciences

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in abstract: canada; Canada-related source title

Abstract: Increased aquatic turbidity often co-occurs with other environmental stressors, such as warming waters and low oxygen (hypoxia), which may impact the behaviour and physiology of aquatic organisms. Using three experiments, we examined whether chronic turbidity exposure affects the standard metabolic rate, hypoxia tolerance, and growth of the Endangered Lake Chubsucker (<i>Erimyzon sucetta</i>) and whether acute exposure to turbidity impacts the behaviour and thermal tolerance of this species. Our results suggest that chronic exposure to turbidity decreases growth and decreases critical oxygen tension (Pcrit) in Lake Chubsucker relative to the chronic exposure to clear water. Across turbidity treatments, the mean Pcrit and oxygen level at loss of equilibrium were 26.3% air saturation and 4.7% air saturation respectively. When Lake Chubsucker were acutely exposed to medium turbidity, they...

item.title: The deglaciation history of Cape Norton Shaw Inlet (eastern Canadian Arctic Archipelago)
item.description:
Authors: Elodie Bracquart, Jean‐Carlos Montero‐Serrano, Patrick Lajeunesse, Guillaume St‐Onge, Luke Copland, Andrew K. Hamilton, Gabriel Joyal

Source: Quaternary Science Reviews

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in title: canadian; Canada-related term in abstract: bc

Abstract: This study presents a geophysical and morphosedimentary framework for the reconstruction of the glacial history of a fjord of Ellesmere Island, the Cape Norton Shaw Inlet. The reconstruction is based on a multimethod approach combining high-resolution swath bathymetry, acoustic sub-bottom profiles, and a dated composite sediment sequence (33BC/05GGC: 01CNS). The presence of at least five grounding-zone wedges and a large cluster of moraines at the inlet entrance suggests a period of glacial stillstands and gradual retreat during the Younger Dryas. A radiocarbon date of 10.4 cal ka BP from the mid-inlet provides a minimum age for the retreat of the Innuitian Ice Sheet during the early Holocene. Mineralogical and geochemical signatures from core 01CNS, along with low sedimentation rate, support the hypothesis of the gradual opening of Nares Strait between 9.6 and 8 cal ka BP. Until 1.9...

item.title: Understanding Daily Cannabis Use among Young Adults in Post-Legalization Québec: Motivations, Consumption Practices, and Control Strategies
item.description:
Authors: Isidora Janezic, Marie-Eve Levasseur

Source: Substance Use & Misuse

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution; Canada-related term in title: québec; Canada-related term in abstract: canada, québec

Abstract: BACKGROUND: In the wake of cannabis legalization in Canada, daily use among young adults remains insufficiently understood. This study explores the motivations, consumption practices, and self-regulation strategies of young adults who use cannabis daily in the region of Capitale-Nationale, Québec. METHODS: Forty-six individuals aged 18-34 who consume cannabis daily participated in semi-structured interviews and completed a sociodemographic questionnaire. Data were analyzed using thematic analysis, guided by the COM-B model (Capability, Opportunity, Motivation-Behavior). RESULTS: Participants reported diverse and evolving motivations for daily cannabis use, including management of mental and physical health, relaxation, spirituality, and enhancement of everyday experiences. Most respondents expressed a deliberate effort to control their use, aligning consumption with daily obligations....

item.title: A Holistic Market Research Analysis of Carbon-Negative Fuel Systems embedding Decision-Making Procedures
item.description:
Authors: Fatima Dargam, Gabriel Steffen

Source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: The document is a short paper for ICDSST 2026 is authored by REACH Innovation together with several European research partners and presents the Market and Competitive Research Analysis (MCRA) developed within the Horizon Europe project NET‑Fuels. The paper explains how decision-support methodologies (SWOT, PESTEL, Porter’s Five Forces) were integrated into a structured workflow to evaluate and position NET‑Fuels’ carbon‑negative fuel technologies within the current technological and regulatory landscape.

item.title: Optimal cell behavior in time
item.description:
Authors: Dafni Giannari, Hidde de Jong, Diego A. Oyarzún, Steffen Waldherr, Agustín G. Yabo

Source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: This is a chapter from the free textbook "Economic Principles in Cell Biology" Microorganisms live in continually changing environments, which require them to develop adaptation strategies. These strategies have been profitably studied under the assumption that microorganisms have evolved to optimize one or several aspects of their adaptive response. The mathematical formalization of this assumption leads to dynamic optimization problems that can be solved by means of techniques from optimal control theory. The chapter discusses three example problems: dynamic optimization of enzyme expression in metabolic pathways, dynamic optimization of coarse-grained models of cellular growth, and dynamic flux balance analysis. The results obtained for these problems illustrate the interest of studying adaptation strategies from the perspective of dynamic optimization, and the strengths and...

item.title: A escola não é um banco: educação bancária, conscientização e a práxis libertadora
item.description:
Authors: Laís Machado Ribeiro Luz

Source: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)

Why included: Canadian-affiliated author or institution

Abstract: Este ensaio analisa a Pedagogia Libertadora de Paulo Freire a partir do conceito central de educação bancária e suas implicações para a produção da alienação escolar. Retomando a conexão entre Freire e a crítica marxista ao trabalho alienado, o texto argumenta que a educação que deposita informação em vez de construir consciência é um instrumento ativo de dominação. A conscientização e a práxis são apresentadas não como ideais pedagógicos abstratos, mas como processos históricos concretos.
